salmon: Salmon is one of the richest sources of omega-3 fatty acids, which support heart and brain health.
Wild salmon gets its pink color from astaxanthin, a powerful antioxidant found in the krill they eat.
turmeric: Turmeric contains curcumin, whose absorption increases 2000% when paired with black pepper.
Turmeric has been used in Ayurvedic medicine for over 4,000 years.
garlic: Garlic contains allicin, which forms when cloves are crushed and has natural antibacterial properties.
Letting crushed garlic sit for 10 minutes before cooking preserves more of its beneficial compounds.
chicken: Chicken breast is one of the leanest protein sources, with about 31g of protein per 100g.
Dark meat chicken contains more iron and zinc than white meat.
egg: Eggs contain all 9 essential amino acids, making them a complete protein source.
The color of an eggshell is determined by the breed of hen and has no effect on nutrition.
avocado: Avocados contain more potassium than bananas — about 485mg per 100g.
Avocados are technically a berry and are one of the few fruits that contain healthy fats.
spinach: Cooking spinach actually increases its iron absorption by breaking down oxalic acid.
Spinach contains lutein and zeaxanthin, which support eye health and may prevent macular degeneration.
oat: Oats contain beta-glucan, a soluble fiber that has been shown to lower cholesterol levels.
Steel-cut oats and rolled oats have the same nutritional profile — only the texture differs.
sweet potato: Sweet potatoes are one of the best sources of beta-carotene, which the body converts to vitamin A.
Purple sweet potatoes contain anthocyanins, the same antioxidants found in blueberries.
broccoli: Broccoli contains sulforaphane, a compound studied for its potential anti-cancer properties.
Steaming broccoli for 3-4 minutes preserves more nutrients than boiling or microwaving.
quinoa: Quinoa is one of the few plant foods that is a complete protein, containing all 9 essential amino acids.
Quinoa is technically a seed, not a grain, and is related to spinach and beets.
blueberry: Blueberries have one of the highest antioxidant levels of any common fruit.
The deep blue color of blueberries comes from anthocyanins, which may improve memory and cognitive function.
ginger: Ginger contains gingerol, which has powerful anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects.
Studies show ginger can reduce muscle pain and soreness by up to 25% after exercise.
lemon: Lemons contain more potassium than apples or grapes.
The citric acid in lemons can enhance iron absorption from plant-based foods by up to 6x.
olive oil: Extra virgin olive oil contains oleocanthal, a compound with anti-inflammatory effects similar to ibuprofen.
Olive oil is the only cooking oil that is made without chemical processing.
tomato: Cooking tomatoes increases the bioavailability of lycopene, their primary antioxidant.
Tomatoes are technically fruits and were once considered poisonous in 18th-century Europe.
almond: Almonds are the most nutrient-dense tree nut, packed with vitamin E, magnesium, and fiber.
Eating almonds with their skin on provides 2x more antioxidants than blanched almonds.
banana: Green bananas contain resistant starch, which acts as prebiotic fiber for gut bacteria.
Bananas are naturally slightly radioactive due to their potassium content (potassium-40 isotope).
rice: Cooling cooked rice in the fridge converts some starch to resistant starch, lowering its glycemic impact.
Brown rice has 3x more fiber than white rice and retains the nutrient-rich bran layer.
yogurt: Greek yogurt has roughly 2x the protein of regular yogurt due to the straining process.
Live-culture yogurt contains probiotics that support gut microbiome diversity.
honey: Honey never spoils — archaeologists have found 3,000-year-old honey in Egyptian tombs that was still edible.
Raw honey contains trace enzymes, antioxidants, and small amounts of B vitamins.
cinnamon: Ceylon cinnamon contains far less coumarin than cassia cinnamon, making it safer for daily use.
Studies suggest cinnamon may help improve insulin sensitivity and blood sugar regulation.
mushroom: Mushrooms are the only plant-based source of vitamin D, and exposure to sunlight increases their content.
Some mushroom varieties contain beta-glucans that may support immune system function.
dark chocolate: Dark chocolate (70%+) contains more antioxidants per serving than most fruits.
The flavanols in dark chocolate may improve blood flow and lower blood pressure.
tofu: Tofu is made by coagulating soy milk, similar to how cheese is made from dairy milk.
Firm tofu provides about 10g of protein per 100g and is an excellent source of calcium.
lentil: Lentils are one of the fastest-cooking legumes and don't require soaking before cooking.
One cup of cooked lentils provides about 18g of protein and 15g of fiber.
bell pepper: Red bell peppers contain nearly 3x more vitamin C than oranges by weight.
Bell peppers change color as they ripen — green is unripe, while red, yellow, and orange are fully ripe and sweeter.
coconut: Coconut water is naturally isotonic and contains electrolytes similar to human blood plasma.
Medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) in coconut oil are metabolized differently than other fats.
beef: Grass-fed beef contains up to 5x more omega-3 fatty acids than grain-fed beef.
Beef is one of the best dietary sources of heme iron, which is absorbed 2-3x better than plant iron.
carrot: Cooking carrots actually increases beta-carotene availability by breaking down cell walls.
Purple carrots were the original variety — orange carrots were bred in the Netherlands in the 17th century.
walnut: Walnuts are the only tree nut that contains a significant amount of alpha-linolenic acid (plant omega-3).
The wrinkly shape of a walnut resembles the brain, and research shows they may support brain health.
peanut: Peanuts are technically legumes, not tree nuts — they grow underground.
Two tablespoons of peanut butter provide about 7g of protein and healthy monounsaturated fats.
cucumber: Cucumbers are 95% water, making them one of the most hydrating foods you can eat.
The phrase 'cool as a cucumber' has scientific basis — the inside can be up to 20 degrees cooler than outside air.
onion: Onions contain quercetin, a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory properties that survives cooking.
Chilling onions before cutting reduces the release of the compound that makes your eyes water.
pasta: Al dente pasta has a lower glycemic index than overcooked pasta because the starch is less gelatinized.
Italy has over 350 recognized pasta shapes, each designed to hold different sauces.
miso: Miso is a fermented food rich in probiotics that support digestive health.
The fermentation process in miso can take anywhere from a few weeks to several years.
